places.name: Red Fort
places.description: A massive 17th-century fortress built from red sandstone by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan. A UNESCO World Heritage site and the symbol of India's independence, where the Prime Minister hoists the flag every August 15th.
places.history: Built in 1639 when Shah Jahan moved his capital from Agra to Delhi. It served as the main residence of the Mughal Emperors for nearly 200 years.
places.famousFor: Mughal architecture, Diwan-i-Aam, Diwan-i-Khas

places.name: India Gate
places.description: An iconic 42-meter-high war memorial arch resembling the Arc de Triomphe, standing at the heart of New Delhi to honor the Indian soldiers who died in World War I.
places.history: Designed by Edwin Lutyens and unveiled in 1931. It houses the Amar Jawan Jyoti and the newly constructed National War Memorial.
places.famousFor: War memorial, Amar Jawan Jyoti, evening strolls

places.name: Qutub Minar
places.description: A soaring, 73-meter-high tower of victory built in 1193 by Qutab-ud-din Aibak. This UNESCO World Heritage site is the tallest brick minaret in the world.
places.history: Construction was started by the founder of the Delhi Sultanate in 1193 and completed by his successors. The complex also houses the mysterious rust-resistant Iron Pillar of Delhi.
places.famousFor: Tallest brick minaret, UNESCO World Heritage, and ancient Iron Pillar.

places.name: Humayun's Tomb
places.description: A magnificent 16th-century garden tomb built for the Mughal Emperor Humayun. Its Persian-inspired red sandstone and marble architecture directly inspired the Taj Mahal.
places.history: Built in 1570 by Humayun's widow, Empress Bega Begum. It was the first garden-tomb on the Indian subcontinent and is a UNESCO World Heritage site.
places.famousFor: Precursor to the Taj Mahal, Charbagh layout, and Mughal architecture.

places.name: Lotus Temple
places.description: An architectural marvel shaped like a blooming lotus flower. This Bahá'í House of Worship is open to all, offering a pristine, silent hall for meditation and prayer.
places.history: Completed in 1986, it was designed by Iranian architect Fariborz Sahba. It is one of the most visited religious buildings in the world.
places.famousFor: Lotus-shaped architecture, Bahá'í faith, and tranquil meditation hall.

places.name: Swaminarayan Akshardham
places.description: A colossal and intricately carved pink sandstone temple dedicated to Lord Swaminarayan, sitting in the middle of 23-acre manicured lawns. Features high-tech exhibitions and an evening water show.
places.history: Inaugurated in 1992, constructed by BAPS using 6,000 tonnes of pink sandstone from Rajasthan without the use of any steel.
places.famousFor: Intricate stone architecture, Sat-Chit-Anand Water Show.

places.name: Jama Masjid
places.description: One of the largest mosques in India, capable of holding 25,000 devotees. Its towering minarets and expansive courtyard offer stunning views of Old Delhi.
places.history: Built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan between 1650 and 1656. It was the principal mosque of the emperor and remains a vital religious center today.
places.famousFor: Mughal architecture, grand courtyard, and views from the southern minaret.

places.name: Chandni Chowk
places.description: A chaotic, vibrant, and historic market area dating back to the 17th century, famous for its narrow labyrinthine alleys, wholesale markets, and legendary street food.
places.history: Designed by Shah Jahan's daughter, Jahanara Begum, in 1650. Once divided by canals to reflect the moonlight, giving it the name 'Moonlight Square'.
places.famousFor: Paranthe Wali Gali, Khari Baoli (spice market), and Kinari Bazaar.

places.name: Rashtrapati Bhavan
places.description: The majestic official residence of the President of India, boasting 340 rooms and vast Mughal Gardens, serving as a masterpiece of British imperial architecture.
places.history: Designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker, it was completed in 1929 as the Viceroy's House before becoming the President's residence post-independence.
places.famousFor: Presidential residence, Mughal Gardens (Amrit Udyan), and changing of the guard.

places.name: Gurudwara Bangla Sahib
places.description: A prominent Sikh house of worship instantly recognizable by its golden dome. Known for its serene sarovar (holy pond) and massive langar serving thousands daily.
places.history: Originally a bungalow belonging to Raja Jai Singh, it was the site where the eighth Sikh Guru, Guru Har Krishan, stayed in 1664 to tend to the sick during an epidemic.
places.famousFor: Golden dome, holy sarovar, and community kitchen (Langar).

places.name: Lodhi Garden
places.description: A lush, 90-acre city park dotted with the dramatic 15th-century tombs of the Sayyid and Lodi dynasties — a favorite spot for morning walkers, picnickers, and photographers.
places.history: The tombs were built in the 15th century. The gardens were landscaped in 1936 by Lady Willingdon and remain one of Delhi's most beautiful green spaces.
places.famousFor: Mohammed Shah's Tomb, Sikandar Lodi's Tomb, and landscaped greenery.

places.name: Hauz Khas Fort
places.description: A trendy, bohemian neighborhood blending centuries-old Islamic ruins and a large medieval reservoir with modern cafes, chic boutiques, and vibrant nightlife.
places.history: The 'Hauz Khas' (Royal Tank) was built by Alauddin Khalji in the 13th century to supply water to the city of Siri. The village grew around the ruins of a madrasa and tombs.
places.famousFor: Deer Park, Hauz Khas Fort ruins, cafes

places.name: National Museum
places.description: India's premier museum offering a deep dive into 5,000 years of Indian history, featuring Harappan artifacts, Chola bronzes, Buddhist art, and intricate miniature paintings.
places.history: Established in 1949, it holds over 200,000 works of art, both of Indian and foreign origin, covering more than 5,000 years of cultural heritage.
places.famousFor: Indus Valley Civilization artifacts, Dancing Girl statue, and Buddhist relics.

places.name: Jantar Mantar
places.description: An 18th-century astronomical observatory featuring massive architectural astronomy instruments designed to observe planetary movements with the naked eye.
places.history: Constructed by Maharaja Jai Singh II of Jaipur in 1724. It consists of 13 architectural astronomy instruments including the massive Samrat Yantra sundial.
places.famousFor: Ancient astronomical instruments, Samrat Yantra, and architectural geometry.

places.name: Raj Ghat
places.description: A serene, beautifully landscaped memorial dedicated to Mahatma Gandhi, featuring a simple black marble square marking the spot of his cremation.
places.history: Mahatma Gandhi was cremated here on January 31, 1948, following his assassination. It is a traditional stop for visiting foreign dignitaries.
places.famousFor: Mahatma Gandhi memorial, eternal flame, and peaceful gardens.

places.name: Purana Qila
places.description: One of the oldest forts in Delhi, built by Sher Shah Suri and Humayun. Known for its massive walls, the beautiful Qila-i-Kuhna Mosque, and evening sound & light shows.
places.history: Built in the 16th century on the legendary site of Indraprastha, the ancient city from the Mahabharata.
places.famousFor: 16th-century fortress, Qila-i-Kuhna Mosque, and Sound & Light show.

places.name: Safdarjung Tomb
places.description: The last monumental tomb garden of the Mughals, featuring an elegant sandstone and marble mausoleum surrounded by palm-lined charbagh gardens.
places.history: Built in 1754 for Nawab Safdarjung, a powerful prime minister of the Mughal Empire. It marks the final phase of Mughal architectural style in Delhi.
places.famousFor: Late Mughal architecture, Charbagh layout, and peaceful gardens.

places.name: Agrasen ki Baoli
places.description: A striking 14th-century historical stepwell hidden amidst the high-rises of central Delhi, featuring 108 steep steps descending into dramatic archways.
places.history: Rebuilt in the 14th century by the Agrawal community tracing its origin to the legendary King Agrasen. Popularized recently by Bollywood films like 'PK'.
places.famousFor: Ancient stepwell, photography, and haunted legends.

places.name: National Gallery of Modern Art
places.description: Housed in the former palace of the Maharaja of Jaipur, this premier art gallery showcases thousands of spectacular paintings and sculptures from modern Indian artists.
places.history: Established in 1954, it houses the finest collection of modern Indian art, including works by Amrita Sher-Gil, Rabindranath Tagore, and Jamini Roy.
places.famousFor: Modern Indian art, Amrita Sher-Gil paintings, and Jaipur House architecture.

places.name: Dilli Haat INA
places.description: An open-air craft bazaar and food plaza where artisans from across India sell authentic handicrafts, textiles, and regional delicacies in a village-like setting.
places.history: Established jointly by Delhi Tourism and the Ministry of Textiles to provide a permanent platform for rural craftsmen to sell directly to consumers.
places.famousFor: Handicrafts shopping, regional Indian cuisines, and cultural festivals.

places.name: Mehrauli Archaeological Park
places.description: A sprawling 200-acre park adjacent to Qutub Minar, containing over 100 historically significant monuments spanning 1,000 years of Delhi's continuous occupation.
places.history: Features ruins from the Khalji, Tughlaq, and Lodi dynasties, including the beautiful Jamali Kamali Mosque and Rajon Ki Baoli stepwell.
places.famousFor: Jamali Kamali Mosque, Rajon ki Baoli, and 1

places.name: ISKCON Temple Delhi
places.description: A grand temple dedicated to Lord Krishna featuring striking architecture, intricate paintings, and the Vedic Expo, known for its vibrant evening aartis and spiritual energy.
places.history: Opened in 1998, this Hare Krishna temple is one of the largest temple complexes in India, blending traditional design with modern technology.
places.famousFor: Hare Krishna chanting, Vedic Expo, and grand Janmashtami festival.

places.name: Garden of Five Senses
places.description: A 20-acre eco-park designed to stimulate all five human senses through themed gardens, sculptures, water features, solar energy parks, and vibrant floral displays.
places.history: Developed by Delhi Tourism and opened in 2003 as a space for public leisure, cultural events, and festivals.
places.famousFor: Sensory gardens, sculptures, Khas Bagh

places.name: National Rail Museum
places.description: A fascinating outdoor museum detailing the 160-year history of Indian Railways, featuring vintage steam locomotives, royal saloons, and a toy train ride for kids.
places.history: Inaugurated in 1977, it houses some of the rarest historical train models, including the Fairy Queen, one of the world's oldest working steam locomotives.
places.famousFor: Vintage steam engines, Fairy Queen, Royal Saloons

places.name: Waste to Wonder Park
places.description: A unique eco-park featuring incredible replicas of the Seven Wonders of the World, entirely created from 150 tons of industrial and automobile scrap metal.
places.history: Inaugurated in 2019, inspired by the movie 'Badrinath Ki Dulhania'. It was built by 14 artists and 70 welders to promote recycling and sustainable art.
places.famousFor: Seven wonders replicas made from scrap, evening illumination.

places.name: Sunder Nursery
places.description: A stunning 90-acre heritage park complex blending beautifully restored 16th-century Mughal monuments with diverse botanical gardens, lakes, and shaded picnic spots.
places.history: Originally the Azim Bagh built by Mughals, it was used as a nursery by the British. Recently restored by the Aga Khan Trust for Culture into a world-class park.
places.famousFor: Restored Mughal tombs, biodiversity, weekend farmers' markets

places.name: Tughlaqabad Fort
places.description: The massive, imposing ruins of a 14th-century fortress city built by Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq. Its sloping rubble-filled city walls stretch for 6 kilometers.
places.history: Built in 1321, it was cursed by the Sufi saint Nizamuddin Auliya to remain deserted. The city was abandoned barely six years after its completion.
places.famousFor: Massive stone fortifications, Tomb of Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq, and Saint's curse legend.

places.name: Connaught Place
places.description: The vibrant commercial heart of New Delhi, recognizable by its iconic circular colonnaded Georgian-style buildings, housing high-end boutiques, vintage cinemas, and trendy restaurants.
places.history: Designed by Robert Tor Russell and completed in 1933, named after the Duke of Connaught. It forms the centerpiece of Lutyens' Delhi.
places.famousFor: Circular colonial architecture, shopping, dining

places.name: National Zoological Park
places.description: A massive 176-acre zoo located next to the Old Fort, home to nearly 130 species of animals and birds from around the world, including the rare white tiger.
places.history: Inaugurated in 1959. It is heavily involved in conservation breeding programs for endangered species like the Royal Bengal Tiger and Indian Rhinoceros.
places.famousFor: White tigers, elephant rides, and 176-acre green habitat.

places.name: Amrit Udyan (Mughal Gardens)
places.description: The soul of the Presidential Palace, this spectacular garden blends Mughal and English landscaping styles, bursting with thousands of rare tulips, roses, and fountains.
places.history: Designed by Edwin Lutyens. It is open to the public only for a few weeks every year during the spring season (Udyan Utsav).
places.famousFor: Seasonal Udyan Utsav, thousands of rose varieties, and musical fountains.
places.bestTime: February to March (Only time it is open)
places.timings: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM (Closed on Mondays, during season)

places.name: Hazrat Nizamuddin Dargah
places.description: The sacred 14th-century mausoleum of the revered Sufi saint Hazrat Nizamuddin Auliya. Famous for its mystical atmosphere and soul-stirring live Qawwali sessions on Thursday evenings.
places.history: Built in 1325, the shrine complex also houses the tombs of poet Amir Khusrow and Mughal princess Jahanara Begum.
places.famousFor: Sufi shrine, Thursday evening Qawwalis, and Amir Khusrow's tomb.

places.name: Chhatarpur Temple
places.description: A sprawling 70-acre temple complex built entirely from pristine white marble, dedicated to Goddess Katyayani. It was India's largest Hindu temple before Akshardham was built.
places.history: Established in 1974 by Baba Sant Nagpal ji, blending both South and North Indian styles of temple architecture.
places.famousFor: White marble architecture, Goddess Katyayani, and massive Navratri celebrations.

places.name: Gandhi Smriti
places.description: The poignant museum and memorial where Mahatma Gandhi spent the last 144 days of his life before his assassination in 1948. Concrete footsteps trace his final walk to the prayer ground.
places.history: Originally the grand residence of the Birla family, it was acquired by the Government of India in 1971 and converted into a National Memorial.
places.famousFor: Martyr's Column, Gandhi's room preserved exactly as he left it, and multimedia museum.

places.name: National Science Centre
places.description: One of Asia's largest science centers, featuring seven floors of interactive exhibits, a 3D theater, and an iconic 'Energy Ball' sculpture, making science fun for all ages.
places.history: Inaugurated in 1992 by Prime Minister P V Narasimha Rao. It is a constituent unit of the National Council of Science Museums.
places.famousFor: Energy Ball, human biology gallery, and 3D dinosaur shows.

places.name: Crafts Museum
places.description: A charming village-themed museum displaying over 35,000 rare traditional Indian crafts, textiles, and wood carvings, complete with live artisan demonstrations and a popular regional cafe.
places.history: Established in the 1950s by Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay to preserve India's fading rural crafts and traditional arts.
places.famousFor: Live artisan workshops, terracotta horses, and Cafe Lota.

places.name: Asola Bhatti Wildlife Sanctuary
places.description: A crucial green lung on the Southern Ridge, protecting 32 sq km of Aravalli forest. It is a haven for leopards, nilgai, and over 200 bird species, featuring the scenic Bhardwaj Lake.
places.history: Notified as a sanctuary in 1986. The landscape includes deep abandoned quartzite mining pits that have now formed beautiful artificial lakes.
places.famousFor: Bhardwaj Lake trekking, bird watching, and Aravalli conservation.

places.name: Coronation Park
places.description: A significant historical park marking the site of the 1911 Delhi Durbar, where King George V proclaimed the shifting of the capital from Calcutta to Delhi. It now houses colonial-era statues.
places.history: Site of three Imperial Durbars (1877, 1903, 1911). It holds the towering marble obelisk marking the exact spot where the King sat, surrounded by statues of British Viceroys.
places.famousFor: 1911 Delhi Durbar site, King George V statue, and colonial history.

places.name: Yamuna Biodiversity Park
places.description: An inspiring 457-acre ecological restoration project along the Yamuna floodplain, featuring wetlands and grasslands that attract thousands of migratory birds during winter.
places.history: Developed by the Delhi Development Authority to restore the lost ecosystems of the Yamuna River basin, serving as a successful model for urban nature conservation.
places.famousFor: Migratory bird watching, wetlands, and ecological restoration.

places.name: Feroz Shah Kotla
places.description: The atmospheric ruins of the 14th-century city of Firozabad. It is famous for a pristine 3rd-century BC Ashokan Pillar and the practice of leaving letters to Djinns in its dark alcoves.
places.history: Built by Sultan Feroz Shah Tughlaq in 1354. He brought the 13-meter high Ashokan Pillar from Topra (Haryana) and mounted it atop a pyramidal structure.
places.famousFor: Ashokan Pillar, Djinn letters, and 14th-century ruins.
places.bestTime: October to March (Thursdays for Djinn rituals)

places.name: Indira Gandhi Memorial
places.description: The former residence of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi, now a museum displaying rare photographs, her personal belongings, and a crystal pathway marking the exact spot of her assassination.
places.history: She was assassinated here by her bodyguards on October 31, 1984. The museum also contains artifacts belonging to her son, Rajiv Gandhi.
places.famousFor: Crystal assassination pathway, personal artifacts, and historical photographs.

places.name: Kartavya Path
places.description: The grand ceremonial boulevard of India, formerly known as Rajpath, stretching from Rashtrapati Bhavan to India Gate. The 3-km-long tree-lined avenue hosts the iconic Republic Day Parade every January 26th.
places.history: Originally designed by Edwin Lutyens as 'Kingsway' in 1931, renamed 'Rajpath' after independence, and rechristened 'Kartavya Path' (Path of Duty) in September 2022.
places.famousFor: Republic Day Parade, ceremonial avenue, Lutyens' Delhi axis.
places.bestTime: October to March (January 26 for Republic Day)

places.name: National War Memorial
places.description: A solemn, beautifully designed memorial honoring the soldiers of India's armed forces who have made the supreme sacrifice since independence. The four concentric circles — named Amar Chakra, Veerta Chakra, Tyag Chakra, and Raksha Chakra — hold the names of 26,466 fallen heroes.
places.history: Inaugurated by the Prime Minister on February 25, 2019. The eternal flame (Amar Jawan Jyoti) was merged with this memorial in January 2022.
places.famousFor: Amar Jawan Jyoti, four concentric circles, 26,466 inscribed names.

places.name: Birla Mandir (Laxminarayan Temple)
places.description: A magnificent Hindu temple built from red sandstone and marble, dedicated to Lord Vishnu (Laxminarayan). Inaugurated by Mahatma Gandhi on the condition that people of all castes would be allowed entry.
places.history: Built by the industrialist B.R. Birla family and inaugurated by Mahatma Gandhi in 1939. Gandhi's condition that it be open to all castes was revolutionary for its time.
places.famousFor: Gandhiji's inauguration, multi-faith shrines, Birla architecture.

places.name: Kalkaji Temple
places.description: One of Delhi's oldest and most revered Hindu temples, dedicated to Goddess Kali (Kalka Devi). The temple is especially crowded during the Navratri festival, with devotees forming enormous queues.
places.history: Believed to have been established by the Pandavas. The present structure dates to the 18th century and sits atop a small hillock from which the area 'Kalkaji' derives its name.
places.famousFor: Navratri celebrations, ancient Devi temple, hilltop setting.
places.bestTime: During Navratri, or October to March

places.name: Hanuman Mandir Connaught Place
places.description: One of Delhi's oldest temples, dedicated to Lord Hanuman, situated right on the bustling Baba Kharak Singh Marg near Connaught Place. The temple's vaulted roof is a distinctive feature.
places.history: Believed to be established during the era of the Mahabharata, making it one of the five temples from Pandava times in Delhi. The current structure was rebuilt during the Mughal era.
places.famousFor: Pre-Mughal era temple, Baba Kharak Singh Marg, Tuesday worship.

places.name: Gurudwara Sis Ganj Sahib
places.description: A highly revered Sikh gurudwara marking the site where the ninth Sikh Guru, Guru Tegh Bahadur, was beheaded by Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb in 1675 for refusing to convert to Islam.
places.history: Built in 1783 by Baghel Singh to mark the exact site of Guru Tegh Bahadur's martyrdom. The gurudwara stands as a powerful testament to religious freedom.
places.famousFor: Martyrdom site of Guru Tegh Bahadur, Chandni Chowk landmark.

places.name: Gurudwara Rakab Ganj Sahib
places.description: A sacred gurudwara where the headless body of Guru Tegh Bahadur was cremated by his disciple Lakhi Shah Vanjara in his own home. Located opposite the Parliament of India.
places.history: Built on the site where Lakhi Shah Vanjara secretly carried Guru Tegh Bahadur's body and cremated it by setting his own house on fire to avoid detection by Mughal authorities.
places.famousFor: Cremation site of Guru Tegh Bahadur, Parliament adjacency.

places.name: Digambar Jain Lal Mandir
places.description: The oldest and most prominent Jain temple in Delhi, featuring stunning red sandstone architecture and a famous bird hospital (Charity Birds Hospital) on its upper floors that treats over 5,000 birds annually.
places.history: Originally built in 1526 during the reign of Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan. The bird hospital on the top floor embodies the Jain principle of ahimsa (non-violence).
places.famousFor: Oldest Jain temple in Delhi, Charity Birds Hospital, Red Fort views.

places.name: Sacred Heart Cathedral
places.description: The most prominent Roman Catholic church in Delhi, featuring striking white-washed Gothic architecture with beautiful stained-glass windows and twin spires. It becomes spectacularly beautiful during Christmas midnight mass.
places.history: Built in 1930 as the seat of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Delhi. Designed by Henry Medd in a Neo-Gothic style with Italian marble interiors.
places.famousFor: Gothic architecture, Christmas midnight mass, twin spires.

places.name: St. James Church
places.description: Delhi's oldest surviving church, a magnificent Renaissance-style structure with a grand dome and Palladian portico. It is one of the most important colonial-era churches in India.
places.history: Built in 1836 by Colonel James Skinner, a legendary Anglo-Indian military commander who founded Skinner's Horse cavalry regiment. It survived the 1857 Indian Mutiny siege.
places.famousFor: Oldest church in Delhi, Colonel Skinner's legacy, Renaissance dome.

places.name: Fatehpuri Masjid
places.description: A grand 17th-century mosque at the western end of Chandni Chowk, built entirely from red sandstone. It stands as the mirror counterpart to Red Fort at the eastern end of the famous bazaar street.
places.history: Built in 1650 by Fatehpuri Begum, one of Shah Jahan's wives. After the 1857 revolt, it was sold to Lala Chunna Mal and only returned to the Muslim community in 1877.
places.famousFor: Red sandstone architecture, Chandni Chowk anchor, Shah Jahan era.

places.name: Shanti Van
places.description: A serene memorial park where India's first Prime Minister, Jawaharlal Nehru, was cremated. The beautifully landscaped garden with flowering trees creates a peaceful atmosphere for reflection.
places.history: Jawaharlal Nehru was cremated here on May 28, 1964. 'Shanti Van' means 'Forest of Peace', reflecting Nehru's love for nature and his vision of a peaceful India.
places.famousFor: Nehru's samadhi, Forest of Peace, adjacent to Raj Ghat.

places.name: Nehru Planetarium
places.description: One of India's premier planetariums, located within the Teen Murti Estate. The dome-shaped theater offers immersive sky shows about astronomy, the solar system, and the universe.
places.history: Opened in 1984 as part of the Jawaharlal Nehru Memorial complex at Teen Murti Bhavan. Named after India's first PM who had a keen interest in science and discovery.
places.famousFor: Sky shows, astronomy exhibitions, Teen Murti Estate setting.

places.name: Teen Murti Bhavan
places.description: The former official residence of India's first Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru, now converted into the Nehru Memorial Museum & Library. The grand colonial bungalow preserves Nehru's study, bedroom, and personal artifacts.
places.history: Originally built as the residence of the Commander-in-Chief of India in 1930. After independence, it became Nehru's home from 1948 until his death in 1964.
places.famousFor: Nehru's residence, memorial museum, Teen Murti statue, library.

places.name: Sulabh International Museum of Toilets
places.description: One of the most quirky and educational museums in the world, chronicling the 5,000-year history of toilets and sanitation from 2500 BCE to modern times. Featured in Time magazine's list of world's oddest museums.
places.history: Founded by Dr. Bindeshwar Pathak in 1992 as part of the Sulabh Sanitation Movement. The museum documents the evolution of sanitation from ancient civilizations to the present day.
places.famousFor: 5,000-year toilet history, world's quirkiest museum, sanitation advocacy.

places.name: Adilabad Fort
places.description: A 14th-century Sultanate fort built as a companion fortress to Tughlaqabad Fort. Connected to the main fort by an ancient causeway, it contains the tomb of Muhammad bin Tughlaq within its walls.
places.history: Built by Muhammad bin Tughlaq around 1325 CE, shortly after he succeeded his father Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq who built the main Tughlaqabad Fort.
places.famousFor: Tughlaq-era ruins, Muhammad bin Tughlaq's tomb, twin fort system.

places.name: Salimgarh Fort
places.description: A 16th-century fort built on an island in the Yamuna River, now connected to the Red Fort. Used as a prison during the British era, it has been witness to key historical events of Delhi.
places.history: Built by Islam Shah Suri in 1546 CE. Later integrated with the Red Fort complex by Shah Jahan. Used by the British as a prison, including for freedom fighters during the independence movement.
places.famousFor: Pre-Mughal Sur dynasty fort, Red Fort connection, freedom fighter prison.

places.name: Zafar Mahal
places.description: The last palace built by the Mughal dynasty, serving as a summer retreat for the later Mughal emperors. It features a grand gateway and the tomb of Bahadur Shah I within the complex.
places.history: Built in the late Mughal period (18th-19th century) as a monsoon palace for the declining Mughal court. The last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar planned to be buried here but was exiled to Burma.
places.famousFor: Last Mughal palace, Bahadur Shah I's tomb, monsoon retreat.

places.name: Begumpur Mosque
places.description: One of the largest mosques built during the Delhi Sultanate period, featuring seven imposing gateways and a massive courtyard. Its scale rivals Jama Masjid but it remains a hidden, unvisited gem.
places.history: Built by Khan-i-Jahan Junan Shah, Prime Minister of Sultan Firoz Shah Tughlaq, in the 14th century. The massive prayer hall and seven-gate design were unprecedented for its time.
places.famousFor: Seven gateways, Tughlaq-era architecture, largest Sultanate mosque.

places.name: Bijay Mandal
places.description: A massive Sultanate-era palace structure believed to be the 'Hazar Sutun' (Palace of 1000 Pillars) described by the famous traveler Ibn Battuta. The terraced ruins offer panoramic views of South Delhi.
places.history: Built by Muhammad bin Tughlaq in the 14th century as part of his capital Jahanpanah. Ibn Battuta, the Moroccan traveler, described its grandeur in his famous travelogue 'Rihla'.
places.famousFor: Ibn Battuta's 1000-Pillar Palace, Tughlaq ruins, panoramic views.

places.name: Jamali Kamali Tomb & Mosque
places.description: A hauntingly beautiful pair of tombs — one of the Sufi poet Jamali and the other of his mysterious companion Kamali — set inside a stunning mosque with intricate colored tile work on its interior ceiling.
places.history: Built in the 1520s–1530s during the Lodhi/Mughal transition period. The tomb interiors feature remarkably well-preserved painted plasterwork with Quranic inscriptions and geometric patterns.
places.famousFor: Sufi poet's tomb, painted ceiling interior, Mehrauli heritage.

places.name: Aravalli Biodiversity Park
places.description: A revived 692-acre urban forest on the Delhi ridge, showcasing the incredible transformation of a degraded mining landscape into a thriving ecosystem with over 300 native plant species and leopard sightings.
places.history: Developed from 2004 by DDA and the Centre for Environmental Management of Degraded Ecosystems (CEMDE). The park has reintroduced species native to the Aravalli ecosystem.
places.famousFor: Rewilded Aravalli landscape, 300+ native species, leopard sightings.

places.name: Sanjay Van
places.description: A 784-acre dense forest reserve on the Southern Ridge, featuring ancient tomb ruins, dense thorny scrub forest, and a rich population of nilgai, peacocks, and over 200 bird species.
places.history: Part of the Delhi Ridge system, this is one of the last surviving remnants of the ancient Aravalli forest cover within Delhi. It contains ruins dating back to the Sultanate period.
places.famousFor: Delhi Ridge forest, Sultanate ruins, birdwatching, nilgai population.

places.name: Nehru Park
places.description: A vast, beautifully landscaped park in the diplomatic enclave of Chanakyapuri. Features a swimming pool, jogging tracks, and regular cultural events and concerts.
places.history: Developed in 1969 and named after Jawaharlal Nehru. The park features a large stone inscription of Nehru's famous Tryst with Destiny speech.
places.famousFor: Tryst with Destiny inscription, cultural events, diplomatic area park.

places.name: Buddha Jayanti Park
places.description: A serene park built on the Delhi Ridge to commemorate the 2,500th anniversary of Lord Buddha's enlightenment. Features a beautiful Buddha statue, a golden pagoda, and Zen-like landscaping.
places.history: Created in 1993 on 90 acres of the Central Ridge near Vandemataram Marg. A golden relic of Lord Buddha from a Thai monastery is enshrined in the park's pagoda.
places.famousFor: Buddha statue, golden pagoda, Ridge forest trails, peaceful ambience.

places.name: Bharat Mandapam
places.description: India's state-of-the-art international exhibition and convention center that hosted the G20 Summit in 2023. The stunning architecture draws inspiration from India's cultural heritage with a massive lotus-shaped structure.
places.history: Inaugurated in July 2023, replacing the old Pragati Maidan exhibition halls. It hosted the G20 Leaders' Summit in September 2023, putting India's modern architectural ambitions on the world stage.
places.famousFor: G20 Summit 2023 venue, lotus architecture, India's largest convention center.

places.name: Ghalib Ki Haveli
places.description: The restored 19th-century residence of legendary Urdu poet Mirza Ghalib in the Ballimaran area of Old Delhi, now functioning as a small museum showcasing memorabilia of his life and works.
places.history: Mirza Ghalib (1797–1869) lived in this haveli during the last years of his life. It was restored by the Delhi government and opened as a museum to preserve the memory of one of the greatest Urdu and Persian poets.
places.famousFor: Mirza Ghalib memorabilia, Urdu poetry heritage, Old Delhi cultural walk

places.name: Parliament House
places.description: The iconic circular Parliament House (Sansad Bhavan) designed by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker, the seat of India's legislative government since 1927. The new Parliament building was inaugurated in 2023 adjacent to the original.
places.history: Designed by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker as part of the New Delhi construction project. The foundation stone was laid in 1921 and the building was inaugurated on 18 January 1927 as the Council House of the Indian Legislature.
places.famousFor: Indian democracy, Lutyens architecture, Central Vista heritage

places.name: Siri Fort
places.description: The ruins of the second city of Delhi built by Alauddin Khalji in 1303 CE. The fort walls and bastions still stand partially, along with the Siri Fort Auditorium complex that hosts cultural events.
places.history: Built by Sultan Alauddin Khalji of the Khalji dynasty in 1303 CE to defend against Mongol invasions. It was named 'Siri' meaning 'head' — legend says the heads of 8,000 Mongol soldiers were buried in its walls.
places.famousFor: Khalji dynasty ruins, medieval fortification, cultural auditorium

places.name: Iron Pillar
places.description: A 7-metre tall iron pillar located within the Qutub complex, dating to the 4th century CE. It is one of the world's foremost metallurgical curiosities — it has remained rust-free for over 1,600 years.
places.history: Erected during the reign of Chandragupta II (375–413 CE) of the Gupta dynasty. The pillar was originally at Vishnupadagiri (now Udayagiri) and was moved to its current location by Iltutmish. Its corrosion resistance has been a subject of scientific study worldwide.
places.famousFor: Rust-free iron for 1600+ years, Gupta dynasty metallurgy, Qutub Complex

places.name: Rajon Ki Baoli
places.description: A 16th-century three-storied stepwell in the Mehrauli Archaeological Park. One of the best-preserved baolis in Delhi, with ornate arches and a mosque on its upper level.
places.history: Built during the Lodi period in 1506 CE by Daulat Khan. The stepwell served as a water source and a community gathering place. Its name translates to 'Stepwell of the Masons.'
places.famousFor: Lodi-era stepwell architecture, Mehrauli heritage trail

places.name: Chor Minar
places.description: A 13th-century circular tower with 225 holes, built by Alauddin Khalji. Legend says the holes were used to display the severed heads of thieves as a warning.
places.history: Built during the reign of Alauddin Khalji (1296–1316 CE). The tower stands as a grim reminder of the harsh punishments of the Sultanate era. The name literally means 'Tower of Thieves.'
places.famousFor: Khalji-era architecture, unique circular tower, historical curiosity

places.name: Mutiny Memorial
places.description: A Victorian Gothic-style memorial on the Northern Ridge near the campus of Delhi University, originally erected by the British to commemorate their soldiers who died during the 1857 uprising. After independence, a counter-plaque was added honoring the Indian freedom fighters.
places.history: Built in 1863 by the British to commemorate the soldiers who fell during the Siege of Delhi in 1857. In 1972, the Indian government added a plaque re-dedicating the memorial to those who fought against colonial rule.
places.famousFor: 1857 Revolt of Independence history, Victorian Gothic architecture, Delhi Ridge

places.name: Khirki Mosque
places.description: A partially covered 14th-century mosque built during the Tughlaq period. Its unique design features a grid of 25 square bays covered by domes, with four open courtyards — unlike any other mosque in India.
places.history: Built by Khan-i-Jahan Junan Shah, the prime minister of Firoz Shah Tughlaq, around 1351 CE. The name 'Khirki' means 'window', referring to the perforated screens used in its construction.
places.famousFor: Unique covered mosque design, Tughlaq architecture, medieval Islamic heritage

places.name: Adham Khan's Tomb
places.description: A 16th-century Mughal-era tomb near the Qutub Minar complex, known for its impressive single-dome structure and octagonal plan. It once served as a rest-house and post office during the British era.
places.history: Adham Khan was the son of Emperor Akbar's wet nurse Maham Anga. He was thrown off the ramparts of Agra Fort by Akbar's orders in 1562 for murdering Akbar's regent Ataga Khan. His mother built this tomb upon his remains.
places.famousFor: Mughal funerary architecture, connection to Emperor Akbar's court

places.name: Okhla Bird Sanctuary
places.description: A 3.5 sq km bird sanctuary at the point where the Yamuna River enters Uttar Pradesh from Delhi. It hosts over 320 species of birds including numerous migratory species from Central Asia and Siberia during winter.
places.history: Designated as a bird sanctuary by the Delhi government under the Wildlife Protection Act of 1972. The Okhla Barrage, built in 1986, created the wetland that became the sanctuary's habitat.
places.famousFor: Birdwatching, migratory birds, wetland ecosystem, photography

places.name: Roshanara Garden
places.description: A Mughal-era garden in North Delhi built by Princess Roshanara Begum, the daughter of Emperor Shah Jahan. It is one of the few surviving Mughal gardens in Delhi.
places.history: Commissioned in the 17th century by Roshanara Begum, who was the younger sister of Aurangzeb. She supported Aurangzeb during the war of succession. After her death in 1671, she was buried in this garden.
places.famousFor: Mughal garden heritage, Roshanara Club, morning walks

places.name: Shahpur Jat Heritage Village
places.description: An urban heritage village tucked behind the Siri Fort ruins, known for its charming mix of medieval ruins, narrow lanes, independent boutiques, designer studios, and quirky cafes — a blend of old Delhi heritage and new creative culture.
places.history: Named after Emperor Sher Shah Suri's father, Shahpur Jat is an old village that grew around the walls of Siri Fort. It has transformed from a rural settlement into Delhi's creative hub while retaining its medieval character.
places.famousFor: Heritage walks, boutique fashion studios, street art, fusion cafes
